title: Beyond the Borderlands: Migration and Belonging in the United States and Mexico By Debra Lattanzi Shutika University of California Press. 2011. 312 pages. $65.00 cloth, $27.95 paper
abstract: Beyond the Borderlands: Migration and Belonging in the United States and Mexico is part of a growing literature on the politics of immigrant incorporation in nontraditional destinations in the United States. As the book stresses, large and small new destinations have been emerging since as early as the 1980s and raise new questions about how immigrants, especially Latino immigrants, make and claim place in communities with little to no experience with foreign-born populations. Drawing on 10 years of ethnographic research, Debra Shutika examines daily life in Kennett Square, Pennsylvania and Textitlán, Mexico–two ends of the translocal migration that has supplied labor to Kennett Square's mushroom industry and profoundly transformed both communities in the process. Through attention to the experiences of nonmigrant Mexicans in Textitlán, Mexican migrants in Kennett Square, and the long-term population they joined there, this book offers much-needed insight into the lived realities of immigrant incorporation in new destinations and the deeply local nature of this process.
